title: Coffee and hazelnut biscotti
subtitle: Twice-baked Italian biscuits with espresso, whole hazelnuts and a clean snap
excerpt: These biscotti use espresso powder to give a pronounced coffee flavour and blanched hazelnuts for crunch. The twice-baked method produces a biscuit that keeps for three weeks and is dry enough to hold its shape in a cup of coffee or a glass of dessert wine. Slicing at a 45-degree angle gives longer, more elegant pieces. The whole batch takes about 1 hour 20 minutes.
sourceNotes: Biscotti di Prato, also called cantucci, are documented in Tuscany from the sixteenth century and appear in Pellegrino Artusi's 'La Scienza in Cucina e l'Arte di Mangiar Bene' (1891). Coffee and nut variations became common in Italian-American bakeries in the late twentieth century and were subsequently adopted in British cafes. The espresso-hazelnut combination is documented in Heston Blumenthal's 'In Search of Perfection' (2006) and in multiple Italian pastry manuals.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a sealed tin for up to 3 months. They do not need thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Biscotti improve with time: keep in an airtight tin and the texture firms further over the first 3 days. They keep for up to 3 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: first bake at 180 degrees C for 30 minutes; second bake at 150 degrees C for 20 to 25 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: Cutting the baked log into individual biscotti between the two bakes. The log must be sliced while still warm and slightly soft from the first bake; once cold it becomes brittle and will crack rather than slice. Use a sharp serrated knife and a sawing motion. Cutting at a 45-degree diagonal angle to the log produces longer slices from the same log diameter, which is why Italian bakeries always cut at an angle. Aim for slices about 8 to 10 mm thick: thinner slices are fragile; thicker slices need longer in the second bake.

content.text: is the key skill here: cut at 45 degrees while the log is still warm, with a sharp serrated knife. Work deliberately and quickly; a cold log will crack across the hazelnuts rather than cutting between them.

Use espresso powder or instant coffee rather than brewed espresso, which would add too much liquid. Two tablespoons gives a strong but not harsh coffee note; reduce to one tablespoon for a more subtle flavour. The coffee intensifies during the second bake.

Heat the oven to 180 degrees C fan. Whisk together the 2 eggs, 150 g caster sugar and 1 tsp vanilla until slightly thickened. Combine the 250 g flour, 1 tsp baking powder, 0.5 tsp salt and 2 tbsp espresso powder. Stir the dry ingredients into the egg mixture to form a sticky dough. Add the 100 g whole hazelnuts and mix until distributed. With damp hands, divide the dough in half and shape each portion into a log about 28 cm long and 5 cm wide on a lined baking tray.

Bake at 180 degrees C fan for 28 to 32 minutes until the logs are firm, set all the way through and slightly cracked on top. The surface should be deep golden brown. Leave to cool on the tray for 15 minutes only, then slice.

Reduce the oven to 150 degrees C fan. Using a sharp serrated knife, slice each log diagonally into pieces 8 to 10 mm thick. Lay flat on the baking tray in a single layer. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes, turning once halfway, until the slices are dry and lightly golden on both sides, with the hazelnut pieces toasted and deep brown at their edges. They will harden further as they cool. Store in an airtight tin.

items.problem: Log spread into a wide, flat slab during baking
items.solution: The dough was too wet or too warm. The logs should be shaped into a relatively neat mound and the dough should be firm enough to hold its shape. Chill the shaped logs for 15 minutes before baking if the dough is very sticky.

items.problem: Biscotti are still soft after the second bake
items.solution: Extend the second bake in 5-minute increments at 140 degrees C. They should feel hard and dry when you take them out; they will not soften further on cooling.

The espresso powder was old or the brand was mild. Use a fresh espresso powder labelled 'instant espresso' rather than standard instant coffee granules, which are less concentrated. Increase to 3 tablespoons if needed.
